content.full_text:
THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA
OFFICE OF THE GENERAL COUNSEL

SEP 2 8 1990
RECEIVED

James E. Holst
GENERAL COUNSEL

September 27, 1990

VIA FEDERAL EXPRESS

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for Formal Advice Letter

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am seeking a formal advice letter regarding participation by
Meredith Khachigian, a member of the Board of Regents of the
University of California, in a number of anticipated activities
of the Board and the Site Selection Task Force in connection with
choosing a site for a new campus of the University of California,
as described below. I am Deputy General Counsel of the Regents
and am authorized by Regent Khachigian to make this request on
her behalf.

This is the second request for an advice letter involving the
"Table Mountain" site in Madera County and the issue of site
visits and public forums now scheduled for October 7-10. Senior
Commission Counsel Joseph Garcia responded to the earlier request
by letter dated September 19, 1990. We recognize that the issues
raised here are complex, and that you are unlikely to be able to
respond in full before the site visits. If at all possible,
Regent Khachigian would appreciate some advice before October 5
as to whether she could avoid any participation in decision-
making and still be physically present at the site visits and
public forums so that she would not lose the valuable information
produced by that process in the event it is determined there is
no disqualifying financial interest in all or some of her future
activities.

300 Lakeside Drive, 7th Floor    Oakland, California 94612-3550    415-987-9800    FAX 415-987-[illegible]

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
September 27, 1990
Page 2


QUESTIONS

In light of the financial interests of Regent Khachigian and the
other facts described below:

     (1) In order to avoid any disqualifying participation in
the event the Commission ultimately determines there is
a conflict, may Regent Khachigian make the site visits
and attend the public forums scheduled for October 7 to
10, provided that she informs the other members of the
site selection process of her non-participation; does not
participates in no votes and expresses no judgments or
opinions, formally or informally, regarding the
suitability or unsuitability of any site; and engages
in no discussion with fellow members of the site
Selection Task Force, other members of the Board,
University employees or members of the public regarding
the suitability or unsuitability of any site until
receipt of the advice letter? Are there other or
different restrictions under which Regent Khachigian
could be physically present at the site visits and
public forums without engaging in activity which would
constitute a conflict? As noted above, Regent
Khachigian would very much appreciate a response to
this question before the first of the planned site
visits even if it is not possible to address the
substantive issues before that time.

     (2) May Regent Khachigian participate in the deliberations
and votes of the Site Selection Task Force in
connection with recommendations it makes to the
President of the University who will, in turn, make a
recommendation to the Board?

     (3) May Regent Khachigian participate in decisions by the
Board to narrow the list of sites and to choose the
final site?

FACTS

1.  Meredith J. Khachigian is a member of the Board of Regents
of the University of California (the "Board").

2.  In November 1988, the Board authorized University of
California President, David P. Gardner, to begin the process of
planning for possible new campuses of the University.

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
September 27, 1990
Page 3


     3.  In March 1989, President Gardner appointed a site selection
task force (the "task force") made up of Regent Khachigian, two
other members of the Board, and six members of the University
faculty and administration.  The task force is charged with
advising the President on the development and application of
criteria and methodology for choosing new campus sites.  The
President considers the recommendations of the task force in
making his recommendations to the Board, which makes the final
decisions.  However, the President's intention and hope is that
the broadly representative task force will reach a consensus
which he can support.  The President substantively reviews the
recommendations of the task force and consults with the chairman
of the Board and the chairs of the relevant Board committees (the
"consulting regents") before determining what shall be forwarded
to the Board.

     4.  At its July 20, 1990 meeting, the Board voted to further
consider eight site areas in the central region of the state for
a new campus.  One of those site areas is the so-called "Table
Mountain" site located in Madera County, immediately adjacent to
the Fresno County line (which is, at that point, demarcated by
the San Joaquin River) and east of Highway 41.  The boundaries of
the originally identified site are shown on Exhibit 1.  However,
the exact boundaries of the Table Mountain "site" are not firm,
but would be carved out of an area of approximately 20,000 acres
should the site survive further review.  There are no current
proposals to move any of the site area to the west side of
Highway 41.

     5.  The University's current plan is to reduce the eight sites
currently under study to two or three in early 1991 and to select
a site by fall, 1991.  The campus is scheduled to open its doors
to its first students in 1998.

     6.  Castle & Cooke is a corporation listed in the most recently
published Fortune magazine as one of the 500 largest
U.S. industrial corporations.  Castle & Cooke owns a number of
operating subsidiary corporations.  Included among these
subsidiaries are S&J Ranch, Inc.; Oceanic Properties, Inc.; and
Dole Fresh Fruit Co.

     7.  Within the Table Mountain site originally identified by the
University there are two 40 acre parcels.  One of the 40 acre
parcels is owned in whole by S&J Ranch, Inc.  S&J Ranch, Inc.
owns a 50% interest in the other parcel.  S&J Ranch, Inc. is a
wholly owned subsidiary of Castle & Cooke, a holding company.
Thus, operating subsidiaries of Castle & Cooke own the equivalent

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
September 27, 1990
Page 4


of 60 acres which are in the currently identified Table Mountain
site.

8.    Operating subsidiaries of Castle & Cooke also own the
following described property lying west of Highway 41 immediately
adjacent to the Table Mountain site.  S&J Ranch, Inc. and Oceanic
Properties own approximately 900 acres outright.  In the same
area S&J Ranch, Inc. also is a general partner in a series of
limited partnerships owning approximately 6,000 acres.  S&J
Ranch, Inc. has a roughly 50% interest in each of the above-
described limited partnerships.  In addition, S&J manages and
farms several hundred acres in which it has no ownership interest
at all, for a fee or percentage of profits.  Attached as Exhibit
2 is a map showing property owned by subsidiaries of Castle & Cooke
in the currently identified site to the east of Highway 41,
and the nearby property across Highway 41.  Thus, subsidiaries of
Castle & Cooke own the equivalent of 3,900 acres across Highway
41 from the Table Mountain site.

9.    Regent Khachigian is informed that the equivalent of 60
acres presently in the currently identified Table Mountain site
is carried on the books of S&J Ranch, Inc., with a value of
approximately $344,000.  The University expects any site chosen
to be a gift to the University.  In the unlikely event the
University were to negotiate to purchase a part of any site, it
would expect to pay no more than fair market value, without any
appreciable attribution to the University's decision to locate
a campus there.

10.   The University's Assistant Treasurer for Real Estate,
Mr. Gary DeWeese, advises that property in the area of the
originally identified Table Mountain site has recently sold in
the range of $15,000 to $20,000 per acre.  Mr. DeWeese is aware
of no reason why the property to the west of Highway 41 would
have a value different from that in the Table Mountain site area,
but the University has no recent sales or option data on those
properties.  Sites become less valuable as one moves north along
Highway 41.  Castle & Cooke and its operating subsidiaries do not
own property in or adjacent to the northern areas of the site.
Thus, their current properties could be many miles from any site
ultimately carved out of the 20,000-acre Table Mountain site.

11.   Regent Khachigian's husband, Kenneth, is an attorney.
Mr. Khachigian has an ongoing attorney-client relationship with
Dole Fresh Fruit Co.  Regent Khachigian's community property
interest in the income received from Dole Fresh Fruit Co. exceeds
$250 in the past twelve months.

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
September 27, 1990
Page 5


12.  Both the originally identified Table Mountain site area and
the nearby property west of Highway 41 shown in Exhibit 2, are
currently zoned for agricultural uses, but are subject to
development pressure because of proximity to the City of Fresno.
Mr. DeWeese advises as follows: that whether or when the county
rezones the property for development is speculative at this time;
such rezoning would likely cause an increase in the value of all
property in the area; it is likely that such rezoning will occur
if the University campus is located on the Table Mountain site;
because of the existing development pressures it may also be
likely that such rezoning will occur even if the site is rejected
as a new campus site, but the decision to rezoned for development
could be delayed should the site not be selected.

13.  Neither Regent Khachigian nor Mr. Khachigian has had any
contact with Castle & Cooke or any of its subsidiaries concerning
the potential for location of a new campus on the Table Mountain
site, other than to seek information with respect to the
locations of property owned by Castle & Cooke and its
subsidiaries.  So far as we are able to determine Castle & Cooke
has not initiated any contact with the University about their
property; all owners of property on all sites have been notified
by the University that their property is under consideration as
part of a possible site.

14.  Mr. Khachigian's attorney-client relationship is not with
any of the subsidiaries that own property in the Table Mountain
site.  Mr. Khachigian reports to the Vice President and General
Counsel of Dole Fresh Fruit Co., a separate corporation
headquartered in Florida.  Neither Regent nor Mr. Khachigian were
aware, until recently, that Castle & Cooke had interests in
property in that area - and were not aware until August, 1990 of
the specific site locations of the property in which Castle &
Cooke has interests.

Absent any disqualifying financial interest, Regent Khachigian
would anticipate participating in the following activities of the
task force and the Board:

     a.  On October 7 to 10, the members of the task force plan
to personally visit each of the eight sites currently
under consideration.  No votes of the task force will
be taken; although the President's staff will discuss
detailed information about the sites and make
recommendations in furtherance of the study under way.
It is expected members of the task force will
informally discuss their observations among themselves
and with staff members of the University.

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
September 27, 1990
Page 6


b.     Coincident with the site visits described above, the
task force will hold two public forums in Modesto and
Fresno.  The task force plans to solicit input from
people in the affected communities.

c.     Pending determination by the Commission of the issues
presented by her, Regent Khachigian would like to
passively attend the site visits and public forums
described above without in any way making,
participating in the making, or using her official
position to influence any University decision which may
have a material financial effect.  In that regard, she
plans to express no judgment regarding the suitability
or unsuitability of any site and to engage in no
discussions pertinent thereto, and to inform each of
her fellow members of the task force of her intention
to so limit her participation pending receipt of a
formal advice letter.

d.     In several meetings this fall and winter, the task
force will consider available information about the
eight sites under consideration.  It is anticipated
that in February or March, 1991 each of the eight sites
will then be subject to a vote by Regent Khachigian and
members of the task force as to whether it should be
recommended to President Gardner.  The task force will
then forward the results of its vote to President
Gardner to use in connection with his consultation with
the consulting regents and his recommendation to the
Board at a meeting early in 1991, when the Board plans
to reduce the eight sites to two or three.  In voting
on whether to forward the sites for further
consideration by President Gardner, the task force will
be informed by a number of reports from outside
consultants with respect to the criteria applicable to
narrowing the sites at this stage.  The professional
staff of President Gardner will also offer its
recommendations to assist the task force in its
considerations.

e.     At a meeting in February or March of 1991 the Board,
including Regent Khachigian, is scheduled to vote to
narrow the eight sites currently under consideration to
two or three for more intensive further consideration.

f.     The current schedule calls for the Board, including
Regent Khachigian, to vote to choose a site for a new

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
September 27, 1990
Page 7


     campus at its November 1991 meeting after considering
     environmental impact reports and additional detailed
     data on the sites remaining under consideration.

     Please let me know if you need further information.  Thank you
     very much.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Gary Morrison
                                        Deputy General Counsel

     kh

     Enc. 2

     cc:  M. Khachigian

THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA

OFFICE OF THE GENERAL COUNSEL

[University of California Seal]

SEP 2 8 1990

RECEIVED

James E. Holst
GENERAL COUNSEL

September 27, 1990


VIA FEDERAL EXPRESS

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for Formal Advice Letter

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
September 27, 1990

In light of the financial interests of Regent Khachigian and the other facts described below:

(1) In order to avoid any disqualifying participation in the event the Commission ultimately determines there is a conflict, may Regent Khachigian make the site visits and attend the public forums scheduled for October 7 to 10, provided that she informs the other members of the site selection process of her non-participation; participates in no votes and expresses no judgments or opinions, formally or informally, regarding the suitability or unsuitability of any site; and engages in no discussion with fellow members of the Site Selection Task Force, other members of the Board, University employees or members of the public regarding the suitability or unsuitability of any site until receipt of the advice letter? Are there other or different restrictions under which Regent Khachigian could be physically present at the site visits and public forums without engaging in activity which would constitute a conflict? As noted above, Regent Khachigian would very much appreciate a response to this question before the first of the planned site visits even if it is not possible to address the substantive issues before that time.

(3) May Regent Khachigian participate in decisions by the Board to narrow the list of sites and to choose the final site?

1. Meredith J. Khachigian is a member of the Board of Regents of the University of California (the "Board").

2. In November 1988, the Board authorized University of California President, David P. Gardner, to begin the process of planning for possible new campuses of the University.

3. In March 1989, President Gardner appointed a site selection task force (the "task force") made up of Regent Khachigian, two other members of the Board, and six members of the University faculty and administration. The task force is charged with advising the President on the development and application of criteria and methodology for choosing new campus sites. The President considers the recommendations of the task force in making his recommendations to the Board, which makes the final decisions. However, the President's intention and hope is that the broadly representative task force will reach a consensus which he can support. The President substantively reviews the recommendations of the task force and consults with the chairman of the Board and the chairs of the relevant Board committees (the "consulting regents") before determining what shall be forwarded to the Board.

4. At its July 20, 1990 meeting, the Board voted to further consider eight site areas in the central region of the state for a new campus. One of those site areas is the so-called "Table Mountain" site located in Madera County, immediately adjacent to the Fresno County line (which is, at that point, demarcated by the San Joaquin River) and east of Highway 41. The boundaries of the originally identified site are shown on Exhibit 1. However, the exact boundaries of the Table Mountain "site" are not firm, but would be carved out of an area of approximately 20,000 acres should the site survive further review. There are no current proposals to move any of the site area to the west side of Highway 41.

5. The University's current plan is to reduce the eight sites currently under study to two or three in early 1991 and to select a site by fall, 1991. The campus is scheduled to open its doors to its first students in 1998.

6. Castle & Cooke is a corporation listed in the most recently published Fortune magazine directory as one of the 500 largest U.S. industrial corporations. Castle & Cooke owns a number of operating subsidiary corporations. Included among these subsidiaries are S&J Ranch, Inc.; Oceanic Properties, Inc.; and Dole Fresh Fruit Co.

7. Within the Table Mountain site originally identified by the University there are two 40 acre parcels. One of the 40 acre parcels is owned in whole by S&J Ranch, Inc. S&J Ranch, Inc. owns a 50% interest in the other parcel. S&J Ranch, Inc. is a wholly owned subsidiary of Castle & Cooke, a holding company. Thus, operating subsidiaries of Castle & Cooke own the equivalent

of 60 acres which are in the currently identified Table Mountain site.

8. Operating subsidiaries of Castle & Cooke also own the following described property lying west of Highway 41 immediately adjacent to the Table Mountain site. S&J Ranch, Inc. and Oceanic Properties own approximately 900 acres outright. In the same area S&J Ranch, Inc. also is a general partner in a series of limited partnerships owning approximately 6,000 acres. S&J Ranch, Inc. has a roughly 50% interest in each of the above-described limited partnerships. In addition, S&J manages and farms several hundred acres in which it has no ownership interest at all, for a fee or percentage of profits. Attached as Exhibit 2 is a map showing property owned by subsidiaries of Castle & Cooke in the currently identified site to the east of Highway 41, and the nearby property across Highway 41. Thus, subsidiaries of Castle & Cooke own the equivalent of 3,900 acres across Highway 41 from the Table Mountain site.

9. Regent Khachigian is informed that the equivalent of 60 acres presently in the currently identified Table Mountain site is carried on the books of S&J Ranch, Inc., with a value of approximately $344,000. The University expects any site chosen to be a gift to the University. In the unlikely event the University were to negotiate to purchase a part of any site, it would expect to pay no more than fair market value, without any appreciation attributable to the University's decision to locate a campus there.

10. The University's Assistant Treasurer for Real Estate, Mr. Gary DeWeese, advises that property in the area of the originally identified Table Mountain site has recently sold in the range of $15,000 to $20,000 per acre. Mr. DeWeese is aware of no reason why the property to the west of Highway 41 would have a value different from that in the Table Mountain site area, but the University has no recent sales or option data on those properties. Sites become less valuable as one moves north along Highway 41. Castle & Cooke and its operating subsidiaries do not own property in or adjacent to the northern areas of the site. Thus, their current properties could be many miles from any site ultimately carved out of the 20,000-acre Table Mountain site.

11. Regent Khachigian's husband, Kenneth, is an attorney. Mr. Khachigian has an ongoing attorney-client relationship with Dole Fresh Fruit Co. Regent Khachigian's community property interest in the income received from Dole Fresh Fruit Co. exceeds $250 in the past twelve months.

12. Both the originally identified Table Mountain site area and the nearby property west of Highway 41 shown in Exhibit 2, are currently zoned for agricultural uses, but are subject to development pressure because of proximity to the City of Fresno. Mr. DeWeese advises as follows: that whether or when the county rezones the property for development is speculative at this time; such rezoning would likely cause an increase in the value of all property in the area; it is likely that such rezoning will occur if the University campus is located on the Table Mountain site; because of the existing development pressures it may also be likely that such rezoning will occur even if the site is rejected as a new campus site, but the decision to rezone for development could be delayed should the site not be selected.

13. Neither Regent Khachigian nor Mr. Khachigian has had any contact with Castle & Cooke or any of its subsidiaries concerning the potential for location of a new campus on the Table Mountain site, other than to seek information with respect to the locations of property owned by Castle & Cooke and its subsidiaries. So far as we are able to determine Castle & Cooke has not initiated any contact with the University about their property; all owners of property on all sites have been notified by the University that their property is under consideration as part of a possible site.

14. Mr. Khachigian's attorney-client relationship is not with any of the subsidiaries that own property in the Table Mountain site. Absent any disqualifying financial interest, Regent Khachigian would anticipate participating in the following activities of the task force and the Board:

a. On October 7 to 10, the members of the task force plan to personally visit each of the eight sites currently under consideration. No votes of the task force will be taken; although the President's staff will discuss detailed information about the sites and make recommendations in furtherance of the study under way. It is expected members of the task force will informally discuss their observations among themselves and with staff members of the University.

b. Coincident with the site visits described above, the task force will hold two public forums in Modesto and Fresno. The task force plans to solicit input from people in the affected communities.

c. Pending determination by the Commission of the issues presented by her, Regent Khachigian would like to passively attend the site visits and public forums described above without in any way making, participating in the making, or using her official position to influence any University decision which may have a material financial effect. In that regard, she plans to express no judgment regarding the suitability or unsuitability of any site and to engage in no discussions pertinent thereto, and to inform each of her fellow members of the task force of her intention to so limit her participation pending receipt of a formal advice letter.

d. In several meetings this fall and winter, the task force will consider available information about the eight sites under consideration. It is anticipated that in February or March, 1991 each of the eight sites will then be subject to a vote by Regent Khachigian and members of the task force as to whether it should be recommended to President Gardner. The task force will then forward the results of its vote to President Gardner to use in connection with his consultation with the consulting regents and his recommendation to the Board at a meeting early in 1991, when the Board plans to reduce the eight sites to two or three. In voting on whether to forward the sites for further consideration by President Gardner, the task force will be informed by a number of reports from outside consultants with respect to the criteria applicable to narrowing the sites at this stage. The professional staff of President Gardner will also offer its recommendations to assist the task force in its considerations.

e. At a meeting in February or March of 1991 the Board, including Regent Khachigian, is scheduled to vote to narrow the eight sites currently under consideration to two or three for more intensive further consideration.

f. The current schedule calls for the Board, including Regent Khachigian, to vote to choose a site for a new

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
September 27, 1990

campus at its November 1991 meeting after considering environmental impact reports and additional detailed data on the sites remaining under consideration.

Please let me know if you need further information. Thank you very much.

QUESTION: In light of the financial interests of Regent Khachigian and the other facts described below:

(1) In order to avoid any disqualifying participation in the event the Commission ultimately determines there is a conflict, may Regent Khachigian make the site visits and attend the public forums scheduled for October 7 to 10, provided that she informs the other members of the site selection process of her non-participation; participates in no votes and expresses no judgments or opinions, formally or informally, regarding the suitability or unsuitability of any site; and engages in no discussion with fellow members of the Site Selection Task Force, other members of the Board, University employees or members of the public regarding the suitability or unsuitability of any site until receipt of the advice letter? Are there other or different restrictions under which Regent Khachigian could be physically present at the site visits and public forums without engaging in activity which would constitute a conflict? As noted above, Regent Khachigian would very much appreciate a response to this question before the first of the planned site visits even if it is not possible to address the substantive issues before that time.

(2) May Regent Khachigian participate in the deliberations and votes of the Site Selection Task Force in connection with recommendations it makes to the President of the University who will, in turn, make a recommendation to the Board?

(3) May Regent Khachigian participate in decisions by the Board to narrow the list of sites and to choose the final site?

Acting General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, California 95814 Re: Request for Formal Advice Letter Dear Mr. Hallabrin: I am seeking a formal advice letter regarding participation by Meredith Khachigian, a member of the Board of Regents of the University of California, in a number of anticipated activities of the Board and the Site Selection Task Force in connection with choosing a site for a new campus of the University of California, as described below. I am Deputy General Counsel of the Regents and am authorized by Regent Khachigian to make this request on her behalf. This is the second request for an advice letter involving the "Table Mountain" site in Madera County and the issue of site visits and public forums now scheduled for October 7-10. Senior Commission Counsel Joseph Garcia responded to the earlier request by letter dated September 19, 1990. We recognize that the issues raised here are complex, and that you are unlikely to be able to respond in full before the site visits. If at all possible, Regent Khachigian would appreciate some advice before October 5 as to whether she could avoid any participation in decision- making and still be physically present at the site visits and public forums so that she would not lose the valuable information produced by that process in the event it is determined there is no disqualifying financial interest in all or some of her future activities. 300 Lakeside Drive, 7th Floor Oakland, California 94612-3550 415-987-9800 FAX 415-987-[illegible] Scott Hallabrin, Esq. September 27, 1990 Page 2 QUESTIONS In light of the financial interests of Regent Khachigian and the other facts described below: (1) In order to avoid any disqualifying participation in the event the Commission ultimately determines there is a conflict, may Regent Khachigian make the site visits and attend the public forums scheduled for October 7 to 10, provided that she informs the other members of the site selection process of her non-participation; does not participates in no votes and expresses no judgments or opinions, formally or informally, regarding the suitability or unsuitability of any site; and engages in no discussion with fellow members of the site Selection Task Force, other members of the Board, University employees or members of the public regarding the suitability or unsuitability of any site until receipt of the advice letter? Are there other or different restrictions under which Regent Khachigian could be physically present at the site visits and public forums without engaging in activity which would constitute a conflict? As noted above, Regent Khachigian would very much appreciate a response to this question before the first of the planned site visits even if it is not possible to address the substantive issues before that time. (2) May Regent Khachigian participate in the deliberations and votes
